title: Barton Nitrite Ester Reaction – Importance and Mechanism
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Overview page for the Barton nitrite ester reaction describing remote functionalization of unactivated aliphatic C-H bonds through nitrite esters under thermal or photolytic conditions. The mechanism proceeds through O-N homolysis, 1,5-hydrogen abstraction, carbon-radical capture by nitric oxide, and tautomerization to γ-hydroxy oximes.
notes: Emphasizes nitrite ester formation from alcohols and nitrosyl chloride, oxygen-centered radical generation, and 1,5-H abstraction logic for remote oxidation.

title: Barton Nitrite Ester Reaction – Synthetic Applications
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Application page for the Barton nitrite ester reaction featuring intermediates in myriceric acid A, 1-dethia-3-aza-1-carba-2-oxacephem, azadiradione, perhydrohistiiricotoxin, and chain-elongated radical-trapping products from remote functionalization chemistry.
notes: Applications show remote oxidation in terpenes and β-lactam settings, product divergence from multiple hydrogen abstractions, and interception of δ-carbon radicals by acrylonitrile.

title: Barton Radical Decarboxylation Reaction – Importance and Mechanism
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Overview page for the Barton radical decarboxylation reaction describing conversion of carboxylic acids into thiohydroxamate esters followed by radical-mediated decarboxylation to replace a carboxyl group with hydrogen or another radical-derived substituent. The mechanism highlights AIBN initiation, tributyltin-hydride reduction, and loss of carbon dioxide from acyl-derived radicals.
notes: The page presents N-hydroxypyridine-2-thione thiohydroxamate esters as radical precursors and focuses on reductive decarboxylation with Bu3SnH/AIBN.

title: Barton Radical Decarboxylation Reaction – Synthetic Applications
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Application page for the Barton radical decarboxylation reaction featuring syntheses of (-)-verrucarol, 10-decarboxyquinocarcin, functionalized tricyclodienones, bridged bromide or selenide cage systems, and a double Barton decarboxylation en route to tyromycin A.
notes: Applications stress late-stage decarboxylative editing of complex natural-product frameworks and trapping of radicals by oxygen, bromine, or selenium donors.

title: Barton-McCombie Radical Deoxygenation Reaction – Importance and Mechanism
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Overview page for the Barton-McCombie radical deoxygenation reaction describing replacement of an alcohol hydroxy group with hydrogen after conversion to a thiocarbonyl derivative, followed by Bu3SnH/AIBN-mediated radical deoxygenation. The mechanism shows xanthate or thioester formation, radical chain propagation, and hydrogen-atom transfer to a carbon radical.
notes: The page explicitly presents xanthate and thioester precursor manifolds and frames the method as a general deoxygenation protocol for secondary and tertiary alcohols.

title: Barton-McCombie Radical Deoxygenation Reaction – Synthetic Applications
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Application page for the Barton-McCombie radical deoxygenation reaction featuring synthesis of the C1-C19 fragment of kabiramide C, tricycloillicinone, (±)-Δ(9,12)-capnellene, (-)-Kishi lactam, and the octenoic acid side chain of zaragozic acid.
notes: Applications highlight late-stage oxygen deletion in complex stereochemical settings and synergy with oxidative cyclization or acylation steps.

title: Baylis-Hillman Reaction – Importance and Mechanism
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Overview page for the Baylis-Hillman reaction describing tertiary amine or phosphine catalyzed coupling of an activated alkene with an aldehyde or imine to form densely functionalized allylic alcohol products. The mechanism involves catalyst Michael addition, zwitterion formation, carbon-carbon bond formation, proton transfer, and catalyst elimination.
notes: The page emphasizes DABCO, quinuclidine, and chiral cinchona-derived catalysts as key promoters for enantioselective and organocatalytic variants.

title: Baylis-Hillman Reaction – Synthetic Applications
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Application page for the Baylis-Hillman reaction featuring routes to epopromycin B precursors, 3-substituted 2H-chromenes, (E)-α-methylcinnamic acids, kuehneromycin A, and salinosporamide A through inter- and intramolecular Baylis-Hillman chemistry.
notes: Applications show catalyst-controlled stereochemistry and extension of Baylis-Hillman adducts into chromene, cinnamate, and β-lactam-containing target scaffolds.

title: Beckmann Rearrangement – Importance and Mechanism
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Overview page for the Beckmann rearrangement describing acid-promoted conversion of ketoximes or aldoximes into amides or lactams through activation of the oxime hydroxy group, anti migration of the substituent, nitrilium-ion formation, and hydrolysis/tautomerization.
notes: The page stresses anti stereochemical migration, relevance to ε-caprolactam production, and compatibility with Brønsted or Lewis acid activation conditions.

title: Beckmann Rearrangement – Synthetic Applications
section_name: VII. Named Organic Reactions in Alphabetical Order
summary: Application page for the Beckmann rearrangement featuring syntheses of (+)-codeine, (-)-ibogamine, (+)-sparteine, and a chiral 4-ethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroquinoline scaffold via cyclic ketone oxime rearrangement to lactams or related nitrogen heterocycles.
notes: Applications include classical and photo-Beckmann variants and underscore ring expansion to lactams followed by reduction or further elaboration.
